frameworks.name: linear ballistic accumulator
frameworks.one_line: evidence-accumulation model decomposing RT into motor and attentional components

frameworks.name: perceptual decision-making
frameworks.one_line: two-choice perceptual task framework used to probe scanner-environment effects

core_question: How does performing a perceptual decision-making task inside an MRI scanner alter behavior and its underlying cognitive components?
key_assumptions: RT and accuracy decompose into latent components recoverable by LBA
the same task structure applies inside and outside the scanner
task requirements modulate attention vs motor contributions
manipulations: testing environment: traditional setting vs inside MRI scanner
key_findings: response times consistently increase inside the MRI scanner
error rates also rise in the scanner but to a lesser extent
LBA fits attribute scanner effects to slower motor component and reduced attentional focus
balance of attentional vs motor effects depends on task requirements
interpretations: scanner environment slows motor response and reduces attentional focus on the task
fMRI behavioral measures may not be directly comparable to lab-setting measures
